_meta.title: Graph Algorithms II — Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A*

questions.question: Dijkstra's algorithm produces incorrect shortest paths when the graph contains:
options: Cycles with total weight greater than zero
Negative edge weights
Parallel edges between the same pair of vertices
Edges with weight zero
questions.explanation: Dijkstra greedily finalizes the vertex with minimum tentative distance, assuming no later path can improve it. A negative edge from an unfinalized vertex can violate this assumption, producing an incorrect (too-large) distance for the finalized vertex.

questions.question: In Bellman-Ford, running a V-th relaxation pass after V-1 iterations detects negative cycles because:
options: The V-th pass finds all shortest paths using exactly V edges
If any distance still improves, some vertex is reachable via a cycle that reduces cost indefinitely
The V-th pass ensures all unreachable vertices are marked infinity
It recalculates the predecessor array to avoid stale pointers
questions.explanation: Without negative cycles, the shortest path to any vertex uses at most V-1 edges, so V-1 iterations suffice. If a distance improves on the V-th pass, there exists a path with V or more edges that is cheaper — this implies a negative cycle along that path.

questions.question: An admissible heuristic in A* search guarantees optimality because:
options: It is always exactly equal to the true shortest distance
It never underestimates the true cost, so the algorithm explores in the correct order
It never overestimates the true cost, so no optimal path is pruned prematurely
It reduces the time complexity to O(V log V) in all cases
questions.explanation: Admissibility means h(u) ≤ true_cost(u, goal). This ensures that f(u) = d(u) + h(u) never exceeds the true cost of the optimal path through u. If a suboptimal path were returned, some vertex on the optimal path would have f-value less than the suboptimal cost, and would have been expanded first — a contradiction.

questions.question: Which algorithm should you choose to detect currency arbitrage opportunities (negative profit cycles) in an exchange rate graph?
options: Dijkstra's algorithm, because it finds shortest paths efficiently
A* search, because it can be guided toward profit
Bellman-Ford, because it detects negative cycles after V-1 relaxations
BFS, because all exchange rates have equal weight
questions.explanation: Currency arbitrage is modeled by negating the log of exchange rates as edge weights. A negative cycle means you can convert through the cycle and end with more than you started. Bellman-Ford detects these negative cycles in the V-th relaxation pass, making it the correct tool.

questions.question: Why can't Dijkstra's algorithm handle negative edge weights?
options: It uses a priority queue that doesn't support negative numbers
It assumes a vertex's shortest distance is finalized when extracted from the queue, which fails if a later negative edge shortens it
Negative weights create cycles that cause infinite loops
The algorithm's time complexity becomes exponential
questions.explanation: Dijkstra greedily extracts the vertex with minimum distance and marks it as finalized. A negative edge from an unvisited vertex could later reduce that distance, violating the greedy invariant. Bellman-Ford handles negative weights by relaxing all edges n-1 times.
